The regression head predicts the distances from a foreground point to the event’s onset and offset." (Classification and Regression Heads)

inferring_method.answer: Activities are inferred using a Transformer-based model that measures the relevance between video and text embeddings, followed by classification and regression heads to predict event boundaries.

Quotes:

- "given a long video, the retriever first selects key frames relevant to the question, which are subsequently provided as input to a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) answerer." (Sec. 6.4)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper does not specify how frames are sampled from videos before being encoded as a modality.
Quotes:
- "CLAMR jointly indexes four modalities: video frames, transcribed speech, on-screen text, and other metadata." (Abstract)

3) input_video_length
Answer: The system processes long-form videos, with experiments conducted on videos up to 60 minutes in length.
Quotes:
- "given a query about a long (up to ∼ 60 minute) video, we use CLAMR to retrieve relevant segments." (Introduction)
- "we test on Long Video Question Answering (QA) tasks using two benchmarks: the long-video subset (30 - 60 minutes in length) of Video-MME and the (900, 3600s] duration group from the dev set of LongVideoBench" (Sec. 6.4)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on contextualizing different modalities rather than modeling spatiotemporal dynamics within the video stream.
Quotes:
- "all input modalities are first concatenated into a single sequence – with visual inputs preceding textual inputs, based on the model’s training regime." (Sec. 3.2)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: Yes, the system uses a visual-only modality in ablation studies, with Qwen-VL-2.5-3B serving as the visual backbone.
Quotes:

- "We use Qwen-VL-2.5-3B as the backbone for CLAMR with VLM, which offers strong multimodal accuracy at a modest size." (Sec. 5)
- "CLAMR Vision" (Table 2)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, the system processes ASR transcripts as text, and an omni-model variant uses Whisper as a direct audio encoder.
Quotes:
- "CLAMR jointly indexes four modalities: video frames, transcribed speech, on-screen text, and other metadata." (Abstract)
- "For the Omni-model variant, we experimented with Qwen-Omni-3B, which utilizes Whisper as its underlying audio encoder." (Sec. 5)

Answer: Not mentioned. Although the paper mentions "ambient audio" conceptually, the implementation only details models for speech processing.
Quotes:
- "Online video web content is richly multimodal: a single video blends vision, speech, ambient audio, and on-screen text." (Abstract)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Yes, the system is used in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline for long-video QA, with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst as the answerer.
Quotes:
- "We demonstrate the downstream benefits of CLAMR's improved retrieval ability on long-video question answering (QA)" (Introduction)
- "we set up a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline: given a long video, the retriever first selects key frames relevant to the question, which are subsequently provided as input to a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) answerer." (Sec. 6.4)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves both entire documents (videos) for the main retrieval task and individual frames for the downstream QA task.
Quotes:
- "Given a query q, the retriever must identify the most relevant document d." (Sec. 3.1)
- "we use CLAMR to retrieve relevant frames" (Abstract)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Relevance is inferred using a modality-wise late-interaction mechanism that computes token-level similarities and selects the maximum score across modalities.
Quotes:
- "we separately compute the late-interaction similarity score for each modality and then select the maximum score." (Sec. 3.4)
- "The similarity is defined as: LI_mw(q,d) = max_{m ∈ M} Σ_{i=1}^{N_q} max_{j=1}^{N_{d,m}} ⟨E_q^{(i)}, E_{d,m}^{(j)}⟩." (Sec. 3.4, Eq. 3)

11) video_representation
Answer: Content is represented via multiple token-level embeddings for each modality, enabling fine-grained late-interaction similarity scoring instead of using a single pooled embedding.
Quotes:
- "late-interaction (LI) models first independently encode queries and retrieval targets, then compute lightweight but fine-grained token-level similarity" (Introduction)
- "A query yields E_q ∈ R^{N_q×D}, where N_q is the length of the query tokens. Each document provides one embedding matrix per modality E_{d,m} ∈ R^{N_{d,m}×D} for m ∈ M." (Sec. 3.3)

Answer: Text query and multimodal video (frames, ASR, OCR, metadata) → Qwen-VL-2.5-3B jointly encodes modalities into token embeddings → Modality-wise late-interaction computes similarity scores → Ranked list of relevant video documents.
Quotes:
- "CLAMR (bottom) jointly encodes all modalities (a) and, via a late-interaction mechanism (b), computes fine-grained, query-token-level similarities that dynamically focus on the most relevant modality" (Figure 1)

video_segmentation.answer: The paper uses pre-segmented key scene clips from the MovieClips YouTube channel and automatically removes fixed-length 10s or 30s outros from each clip.
quotes: Instead of considering the entire movie, we propose to learn from the 'key scenes' of the movie, providing a condensed look at the full storyline." (Abstract)

...videos from the MovieClips channel on YouTube[1] , which contains the key scenes or clips from numerous movies, are also accompanied by a semantic text description describing the content of each clip." (Section 1)

Each video is accompanied by an outro at the end of the clip which contains some advertising... This is automatically removed by using the observation that each outro has a consistent length of either 10s (if the clip is uploaded before May 2017) or 30s if uploaded after." (Section 3.1)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Frames are sampled at 25 fps for RGB object features and 1 fps for scene features, while every frame is processed for face detection.
quotes: RGB object frame-level embeddings... Frames are extracted at 25 fps, where each frame is resized to 224×224 pixels." (Section 5.3)
Scene features... applied to 224×224 pixel centre crops of frames extracted at 1fps." (Section 5.3)
Face embeddings for each face track are extracted in three stages: (1) Each frame is passed through a dual shot face detector..." (Section 5.3)

input_video_length.answer: The system processes key scene clips with a median length of 132 seconds, condensed from full-length movies; no processed video is over 60 minutes.
quotes: Each around two minutes in length, the clips contain the same rich and complex story as full-length films but an order of magnitude shorter." (Section 3)
...with just the key scenes, each movie has been condensed into roughly 20 minutes each." (Section 3)
Median clip len. (secs) | 132" (Table 2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using motion embeddings generated from the I3D inception model.
quotes: Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model [53] trained on Kinetics [15], following the procedure described by [53]." (Section 5.3)

visual_analysis.answer: The model is multi-modal, using visual backbones including SENet-154 for objects, I3D for motion, SENet50 for faces, and DenseNet161 for scenes.
quotes: RGB object frame-level embeddings of the visual data are generated with an SENet-154 model [52] pretrained on ImageNet for the task of image classification." (Section 5.3)
Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model [53] trained on Kinetics [15]..." (Section 5.3)
Each box is then passed through an SENet50 [56] trained on the VGGFace2 dataset [46] for the task of face verification, to extract a facial feature embedding..." (Section 5.3)
Scene features of 2208 dimensions are encoded using a DenseNet161 model [57] pretrained on the Places365 dataset [58]..." (Section 5.3)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: The model uses YouTube closed captions (subtitles), which are encoded using BERT embeddings.
quotes: For each video, we also download the YouTube closed captions, these are a mix of high quality, human generated subtitles and automatic captions." (Section 3.1)
Subtitles are encoded using BERT embeddings [47] averaged across all words." (Section 5.3)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned; the system is designed for text-to-video retrieval, not interactive question answering.

retrieval_level.answer: The system performs text-to-video retrieval, returning a ranked list of video clips (key scenes).
quotes: The goal here is to retrieve the correct 'key scene' over all movies in the dataset, given just the high level description." (Section 4)
On the left, we provide the input query, and on the right, we show the top 4 video clips retrieved by our model on the CMD test set." (Figure 5 caption)

inferring_method.answer: Roles and activities are represented as embeddings from pre-trained models (e.g., I3D for motion, SENet50 for faces) which are combined for retrieval via a learned similarity score.
quotes: Character IDs are then assigned to face-tracks in the video dataset when the similarity between the embeddings from the face tracks and the embeddings in the CEB (using cosine similarity) is above a certain threshold." (Section 3.1)

...we encode each video as a combination of different streams of descriptors. Each descriptor is a semantic representation of the video learnt by individual experts (that encode concepts such as scenes, faces, actions, objects and the content of conversational speech from subtitles)." (Section 4)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented via a mixture of expert embeddings for different modalities (face, subtitles, objects, motion, scene), which are combined into a single video embedding.
quotes: ...we encode each video as a combination of different streams of descriptors. Each descriptor is a semantic representation of the video learnt by individual experts (that encode concepts such as scenes, faces, actions, objects and the content of conversational speech from subtitles)." (Section 4)

Inspired by [13], we base our network architecture on a mixture of 'expert' embeddings model, wherein a separate model is learnt for each expert, which are then combined in an end-to-end trainable fashion using weights that depend on the input caption." (Section 4)

model_pipeline.answer: A text query and video clips are input to pre-trained expert feature extractors, then a Mixture of Embedding Experts model with a Contextual Boost Module computes a similarity score for ranking and retrieval.
quotes: CBM receives contextual video features (which are previous clips from the same movie) to improve the multimodal encoding of the target video clip. The expert features are extracted using pre-trained models for speech, motion, faces, scenes and objects." (Figure 4 caption)

dataset.answer: The paper introduces the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD) with 3,605 movies, sourced from YouTube; pre-training of experts uses ImageNet, Kinetics, VGGFace2, and Places365.
quotes: We create the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD) consisting of the key scenes from over 3K movies: each key scene is accompanied by a high level semantic description of the scene, character face-tracks, and metadata about the movie." (Abstract)

CMD (Ours) | 3,605 | 1,270 | X | Descriptions, metadata,
character IDs, plots" (Table 1)
Data, code, models and features can be found at <https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/condensed-movies/>." (Section 1)

comparisons.answer: The proposed MoEE + CBM model is compared to E2EWS, CE, and MoEE baselines on the CMD dataset, improving Recall@1 by 0.9% and reducing Median Rank from 65 to 50 over the MoEE baseline.

hyperparameters.answer: The model was trained with the Adam optimizer, a learning rate of 0.001, batch size of 32, a projection dimension of 512, and 10 NetVLAD clusters.
quotes: Optimization is performed with Adam [60], using a learning rate of 0.001, and a batch size of 32." (Section 5.3)
The margin hyperparameter m for the bidirectional ranking loss is set to a value of 0.121, the common projection dimension D to 512, and the description NetVLAD clusters to 10." (Section 5.3)
For CBM, we select the number of past and future context videos to be N=3..." (Section 5.3)

repository.answer: A repository link is provided for data, code, models, and features.
quotes: Data, code, models and features can be found at <https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/condensed-movies/>." (Section 1)

authors.answer: The authors are from the Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford.
quotes: Max Bain, Arsha Nagrani, Andrew Brown, and Andrew Zisserman" (Title section)

paper_file: ContextIQ A Multimodal Expert-Based Video Retrieval System for Contextual Advertising.md

video_segmentation.answer: Long-form content is segmented into shorter scenes using PySceneDetect, and for embedding generation, videos are split into fixed 15-second non-overlapping time segments.
quotes: We found that splitting the video into fixed non-overlapping time segments, each containing T frames, enhances both retrieval accuracy and localization." (Sec. 3.1.1)

Processing Long-Form content. Long-form content, such as movies and shows, is processed by ContextIQ by breaking them into shorter videos using scene detection. We use PySceneDetect [16] for scene detection with default parameters." (Sec. 6)

frame_sampling_method.answer: The system samples one out of ten frames for video embedding generation and samples predictions from every 10th frame for place and object detection.
quotes: We split the video into equal segments of 15 seconds each and sample one out of ten frames for embedding generation (ref. Sec. 3.1.1)." (Sec. 4.1)
For both place and object detection, predictions are sampled from every 10th frame." (Sec. 4.2)

input_video_length.answer: The system is designed for long-form content like movies and TV shows, which are processed into scenes averaging 30 seconds, with analysis performed on 15-second segments.
quotes: With capabilities for long-form content processing, modularity for real-time ad serving, and robust brand safety measures, it integrates seamlessly into the advertising ecosystem..." (Sec. 1)
Processing Long-Form content. Long-form content, such as movies and shows, is processed by ContextIQ by breaking them into shorter videos using scene detection." (Sec. 6)

...we utilize an internal dataset (Val-2) comprising over 2,000 long-form videos (movies, TV, and OTT contents) processed through our ContextIQ system (ref. Sec. 6) to generate over 100,000 scenes (videos), averaging 30 seconds in duration." (Sec. 5.2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: The system uses a fine-tuned VideoMAE2 model for action recognition and applies temporal mean pooling to aggregate frame-level features into segment embeddings.
quotes: We use a fine-tuned video masked autoencoder model (VideoMAE2) [67] on the Kinetics 710 dataset [14, 15, 35] for video action recognition." (Sec. 4.2)

The aggregation functions $\mathcal{A}_a$ and $\mathcal{A}_v$ used during audio and video embedding generation (Sec. 3.1) are temporal mean pooling functions, shown to be effective over other temporal aggregation techniques [10,46]." (Sec. 4.1)

visual_analysis.answer: The system uses a visual modality with a pre-trained BLIP2 Qformer as the image encoder, YOLOv5 for object detection, and a fine-tuned ResNet50 for place detection.
quotes: The vision-text model $f_{\theta_1}$ is a pre-trained BLIP2 Qformer [42], and we use the implementation provided by LAVIS [40]." (Sec. 4.1)
We use the YOLOv5 model [64], trained on the Objects 365 dataset [56], to detect objects within videos with an IOU threshold of 0.45 and a confidence threshold of 0.35." (Sec. 4.2)
For place detection, we finetune a ResNet50 model [32] on the Places365 dataset [76]." (Sec. 4.2)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: The system encodes video captions (transcripts) using a pre-trained MPNet model to incorporate speech information.
quotes: To incorporate textual and speech information from a video, we encode the caption (transcript) $c_i$ for a given video using a text encoder $h_{\theta_3}$, resulting in the caption feature embedding of the video..." (Sec. 3.1.3)
The text encoder $h_{\theta_3}$ is a pre-trained MPNet model [58] fine-tuned on a set of 1 billion text-text pairs as described by Reimers et al [55]." (Sec. 4.1)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: The system uses the CLAP model to encode non-verbal audio elements like sound effects and music from 5-second audio chunks.
quotes: To capture the non-verbal audio elements in a video, such as sound effects, music, and ambient noise, we utilize the audio encoder $g_{\theta_2}$ from an audio-text model [71]." (Sec. 3.1.2)

The audio encoder $g_{\theta_2}$ is the CLAP [71] model as implemented in [2]. Since CLAP is trained on 5-second audio segments, we divide the audio into 5-second chunks $(a_i^k)$ for inference." (Sec. 4.1)

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves relevant video scenes for contextual ad placement, which are stored in a scene-to-context lookup database for an ad serving system.
quotes: ...ContextIQ's multimodal search (ref. Sec. 3.2) identifies scenes where creatives can be contextually served." (Sec. 6)

The selected scenes are stored in the scene-to-context lookup DB. When a viewer is watching the TV, the ad gateway looks up the scene to context lookup DB to find out the relevant context for showing advertisements..." (Sec. 6)

inferring_method.answer: Roles and activities are inferred using specialized expert models: VideoMAE2 for actions, YOLOv5 for objects, ResNet50 for places, RoBERTa for named entities, and an ensemble of LLAMA 3 8B and BERT for hate speech.
quotes: We use a fine-tuned video masked autoencoder model (VideoMAE2) [67] on the Kinetics 710 dataset [14, 15, 35] for video action recognition." (Sec. 4.2)
For named entity recognition, we utilize the text-based RoBERTa model, en\_core\_web\_trf from Spacy [33] to extract the named entities from the captions of each video scene." (Sec. 4.2)
For hate speech detection, we use a weighted ensemble of two models. First, we use a pre-trained LLAMA 3 8B Instruct model [7]... Secondly, we use a pre-trained BERT classifier [36]..." (Sec. 4.2)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented via modality-specific embeddings for video, audio, captions, and metadata, which are stored in a multimodal database. Metadata is first converted into a structured sentence before encoding.
quotes: ContextIQ utilizes modality-specific experts—video, audio, transcript (captions), and metadata such as objects, actions, emotion, etc.—to create semantically rich video representations." (Abstract)
These embeddings are then stored in a multimodal embeddings database." (Sec. 3)

After inference with the task-specific models, we store the extracted information into a metadata sentence $m_i$. This sentence captures objects, places, actions, emotions, named entities and flags any profanity, hate speech, or offensive language." (Sec. 3.1.4)

model_pipeline.answer: Input videos are processed by expert models to generate parallel embeddings for video, audio, captions, and metadata; these are then searched against a text query, with results aggregated and filtered for brand safety to retrieve relevant scenes.
quotes: Input videos are processed by the metadata extracting module, which uses expert models for extracting objects, actions, places, etc., and converts it into a metadata sentence $(m_i)$. Four modality encoders then encode the video frames, audio, caption (transcripts) and metadata in parallel and store the embeddings in a multimodal embeddings DB." (Figure 1 caption)

Text query t is first encoded by different text encoders and the multimodal embedding DB is searched to find similar videos. The aggregation module combines the results obtained from different modalities, and the final results are obtained after applying brand-safety filters..." (Figure 2 caption)

dataset.answer: The system is evaluated on MSR-VTT (1kA subset) and Condensed Movies, and introduces two new datasets: Val-1 (500 public movie clips) and Val-2 (an internal dataset of over 2,000 long-form videos).
quotes: ...we show the efficacy of the proposed approach in comparison to state-of-the-art video retrieval methods on two public datasets, MSR-VTT [73] and Condensed Movies [9]." (Sec. 4.3)

Furthermore, to show that our system performs well for contextual advertisement targeting, we manually collected a set of 500 movie clips of different genres from YouTube corresponding to different potential advertisement categories. We call this dataset Val-1." (Sec. 4.3)
...we utilize an internal dataset (Val-2) comprising over 2,000 long-form videos (movies, TV, and OTT contents) processed through our ContextIQ system..." (Sec. 5.2)

comparisons.answer: The system is compared to LanguageBind and Google Vertex API on MSR-VTT; TwelveLabs Marengo on Condensed Movies; and CLIP, LanguageBind, and One-Peace on the Val-1 dataset, achieving better or comparable results.
quotes: Tab. 1 shows the performance of the proposed approach on the MSR-VTT dataset [73] compared to a stateof-the-art jointly trained multimodal model (LanguageBind [77]) and a popular industry solution for video retrieval (Google's Vertex API [28])." (Sec. 5.1)
As shown in Tab. 2, for the Condensed Movies dataset [9], we compare the results of our proposed technique with TwelveLabs, utilizing their Marengo API [63]..." (Sec. 5.1)
Tab. 3 shows the performance of the proposed approach as compared to different approaches on our curated dataset (Val-1 as described in Sec. 4.3)." (Sec. 5.1)

hyperparameters.answer: Key parameters include 15-second video segments, 5-second audio chunks, a 0.45 IOU and 0.35 confidence threshold for object detection, and a 0.3 softmax score threshold for place detection.
quotes: We split the video into equal segments of 15 seconds each and sample one out of ten frames for embedding generation (ref. Sec. 3.1.1)." (Sec. 4.1)
...we divide the audio into 5-second chunks $(a_i^k)$ for inference." (Sec. 4.1)
We use the YOLOv5 model [64]... to detect objects within videos with an IOU threshold of 0.45 and a confidence threshold of 0.35." (Sec. 4.2)
Top predictions from these frames with softmax scores above 0.3 are considered..." (Sec. 4.2)

environment.answer: All experiments were conducted using PyTorch on four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs.
quotes: We use PyTorch [8] for all our model implementations, and we use four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs to run all our experiments." (Sec. 4.1)

repository.answer: A GitHub repository is provided containing the datasets, retrieval queries, and annotations.
quotes: We release our datasets, retrieval queries and annotations publicly at https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper." (Sec. 1)

authors.answer: The authors are affiliated with Anoki Inc. and the University of Southern California; the venue and year are not mentioned.
quotes: Ashutosh Chaubey∗†, Anoubhav Agarwaal , Sartaki Sinha Roy , Aayush Agrawal , Susmita Ghose Anoki Inc. University of Southern California" (Title Block)
Work done while employed at Anoki Inc." (Footnote)

Answer: Roles and activities are inferred using specialized expert models: VideoMAE2 for actions, YOLOv5 for objects, ResNet50 for places, RoBERTa for named entities, and an ensemble of LLAMA 3 8B and BERT for hate speech.
Quotes:
- "We use a fine-tuned video masked autoencoder model (VideoMAE2) [67] on the Kinetics 710 dataset [14, 15, 35] for video action recognition." (Sec. 4.2)
- "For named entity recognition, we utilize the text-based RoBERTa model, en\_core\_web\_trf from Spacy [33] to extract the named entities from the captions of each video scene." (Sec. 4.2)
- "For hate speech detection, we use a weighted ensemble of two models. First, we use a pre-trained LLAMA 3 8B Instruct model [7]... Secondly, we use a pre-trained BERT classifier [36]..." (Sec. 4.2)

11) video_representation
Answer: Video content is represented via modality-specific embeddings for video, audio, captions, and metadata, which are stored in a multimodal database. Metadata is first converted into a structured sentence before encoding.
Quotes:
- "ContextIQ utilizes modality-specific experts—video, audio, transcript (captions), and metadata such as objects, actions, emotion, etc.—to create semantically rich video representations." (Abstract)
- "These embeddings are then stored in a multimodal embeddings database." (Sec. 3)
- "After inference with the task-specific models, we store the extracted information into a metadata sentence $m_i$. This sentence captures objects, places, actions, emotions, named entities and flags any profanity, hate speech, or offensive language." (Sec. 3.1.4)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Input videos are processed by expert models to generate parallel embeddings for video, audio, captions, and metadata; these are then searched against a text query, with results aggregated and filtered for brand safety to retrieve relevant scenes.
Quotes:
- "Input videos are processed by the metadata extracting module, which uses expert models for extracting objects, actions, places, etc., and converts it into a metadata sentence $(m_i)$. Four modality encoders then encode the video frames, audio, caption (transcripts) and metadata in parallel and store the embeddings in a multimodal embeddings DB." (Figure 1 caption)
- "Text query t is first encoded by different text encoders and the multimodal embedding DB is searched to find similar videos. The aggregation module combines the results obtained from different modalities, and the final results are obtained after applying brand-safety filters..." (Figure 2 caption)

13) dataset
Answer: The system is evaluated on MSR-VTT (1kA subset) and Condensed Movies, and introduces two new datasets: Val-1 (500 public movie clips) and Val-2 (an internal dataset of over 2,000 long-form videos).
Quotes:
- "...we show the efficacy of the proposed approach in comparison to state-of-the-art video retrieval methods on two public datasets, MSR-VTT [73] and Condensed Movies [9]." (Sec. 4.3)
- "Furthermore, to show that our system performs well for contextual advertisement targeting, we manually collected a set of 500 movie clips of different genres from YouTube corresponding to different potential advertisement categories. We call this dataset Val-1." (Sec. 4.3)
- "...we utilize an internal dataset (Val-2) comprising over 2,000 long-form videos (movies, TV, and OTT contents) processed through our ContextIQ system..." (Sec. 5.2)

14) comparisons
Answer: The system is compared to LanguageBind and Google Vertex API on MSR-VTT; TwelveLabs Marengo on Condensed Movies; and CLIP, LanguageBind, and One-Peace on the Val-1 dataset, achieving better or comparable results.
Quotes:
- "Tab. 1 shows the performance of the proposed approach on the MSR-VTT dataset [73] compared to a stateof-the-art jointly trained multimodal model (LanguageBind [77]) and a popular industry solution for video retrieval (Google's Vertex API [28])." (Sec. 5.1)
- "As shown in Tab. 2, for the Condensed Movies dataset [9], we compare the results of our proposed technique with TwelveLabs, utilizing their Marengo API [63]..." (Sec. 5.1)
- "Tab. 3 shows the performance of the proposed approach as compared to different approaches on our curated dataset (Val-1 as described in Sec. 4.3)." (Sec. 5.1)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Key parameters include 15-second video segments, 5-second audio chunks, a 0.45 IOU and 0.35 confidence threshold for object detection, and a 0.3 softmax score threshold for place detection.
Quotes:
- "We split the video into equal segments of 15 seconds each and sample one out of ten frames for embedding generation (ref. Sec. 3.1.1)." (Sec. 4.1)
- "...we divide the audio into 5-second chunks $(a_i^k)$ for inference." (Sec. 4.1)
- "We use the YOLOv5 model [64]... to detect objects within videos with an IOU threshold of 0.45 and a confidence threshold of 0.35." (Sec. 4.2)
- "Top predictions from these frames with softmax scores above 0.3 are considered..." (Sec. 4.2)

16) environment
Answer: All experiments were conducted using PyTorch on four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs.
Quotes:
- "We use PyTorch [8] for all our model implementations, and we use four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs to run all our experiments." (Sec. 4.1)

17) repository
Answer: A GitHub repository is provided containing the datasets, retrieval queries, and annotations.
Quotes:
- "We release our datasets, retrieval queries and annotations publicly at https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper." (Sec. 1)

18) authors
Answer: The authors are affiliated with Anoki Inc. and the University of Southern California; the venue and year are not mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Ashutosh Chaubey∗†, Anoubhav Agarwaal , Sartaki Sinha Roy , Aayush Agrawal , Susmita Ghose Anoki Inc. University of Southern California" (Title Block)
- "Work done while employed at Anoki Inc." (Footnote)
